text: This video demonstrates a laparoscopic assisted right colectomy and cholecystectomy for a neuroendocrine tumor by Dr. Bhiman Khalil. Neuroendocrine tumors are rare, occurring in one to two million people. They are comprised of embryonic cells related to both endocrine producing cells and nerve cells. They can either be benign or malignant. They can be located anywhere in the body, but most commonly occur in the lungs, the brain and the GI tract.

Pathologists recognize five commonest types, pheochromocytoma, Merker cell, carcinoid, thymoma, and thyroid carcinoma. Clinical symptoms are usually not present, but specific symptoms are sometimes produced by carcinoids by secreting an as yet unidentified hormone producing most commonly a distinctive flushing syndrome, diarrhea, heart valvular lesions, or cramping. Insulinomas by secreting insulin producing low blood glucose levels, recurrent headache, lethargy, impaired vision, seizures, comma, and permanent neurological damage. Gastrinomas, by secreting gastrin, producing severe ulcer disease, typically part of the Zollinger-Ellison syndrome with abdominal pains and diarrhea. Glucogonomas by producing decreased blood levels of amino acids, anemia, diarrhea, and weight loss. Pheochromocytomas by secreting epinephrine. This can be associated with skin sensations, flank pain, elevated heart rate, elevated blood pressure, palpitations, excessive sweating or headaches.

VIP omas, producing vasoactive intestinal peptide, that is the VIP, can produce watery diarrhea, low blood potassium, and loss of gastric acid. Somatostatin omas, by producing somatostatin, producing a clinical pentad of diabetes mellitus, cholelithiasis, weight loss, steatorrhea, and diarrhea. sometimes with hypochlorhydria and achlorhydria.

Diagnoses of neuroendocrine tumors can be made in several ways. Imaging with CAT scans, MRIs, and radioactive octreotide scan. Endoscopy using colonoscopy, gastroscopy, bronchoscopy, and endoscopic ultrasound. And finally, hormone assays.

Treatment consists of a combination of surgery, chemotherapy, radionucleotide therapy, and or radiation therapy. This patient presented with blood in the stool and underwent a colonoscopy which showed a tumor of the transverse colon. Pathology showed it to be a malignant neuroendocrine tumor.

Serial CAT scan images display a normal liver followed by the primary colon lesion, highlighted by a yellow circle. The laparoscopic dissection commences with the lysis of adhesions from prior surgery, proceeding across the gastrocolic ligament, around the hepatic flexure, down the right gutter, and finally around the cecum and appendix. Adhesions from the small bowel to the abdominal wall are being dissected using a hook cautery to access the transverse colon.

The surgeon begins the dissection to separate the colon from the stomach, lifting the omentum to reveal the colon. The endoscopic tattoo marking the tumor site is visible, and the tumor is palpated with an instrument. The gastrocolic ligament is opened, exposing the lesser space behind the stomach. The dissection extends to connect with the previous hepatic flexure dissection. Upon lifting the transverse colon, the tumor is observed growing through the serosa, and a clear view of the hepatic flexure and cecum is achieved. Dissection continues around the base of the cecum and appendix. The colon is prepared for delivery through a small abdominal incision for resection and anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an incidental cholecystectomy for symptomatic gallstones. The gallbladder is grasped and retracted to expose the base, allowing visualization of the cystic artery and cystic duct, which are identified, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon begins the dissection by opening the peritoneal covering at the base. The area is opened transversely, and dissection proceeds superiorly to expose the cystic artery area. The cystic duct is progressively encircled, and a small tear in the gallbladder wall is clipped to prevent bile and stone leakage. The cystic duct is clipped twice on the common duct side and divided. Dissection continues along the undersurface to encircle the cystic artery, which is then clearly identified, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon frees the gallbladder from its hepatic attachments and amputates it from the liver surface, holding it with a clamp. A small upper abdominal incision is made to remove both the gallbladder and the right colon. The gross specimen reveals the gallbladder and right colon, with a closer view showing serosal invasion by the tumor. Upon opening the specimen, the primary tumor is visible.
